title: Prepare Your Accelerometer Files
text: Let’s look one example files ships moover. CQU-style file expected time column plus x, y, z. moover converts file internal standard format: id t_unix_ms x y z create five columns CQU-style file. moover creates import.
code: library(moover)  # Find one example CQU-style accelerometer file. cqu_file <- system.file(   "extdata", "example_workspace", "data_raw", "demo-A01_cquFormat.csv",   package = "moover" )  # Preview the first few rows. head(read.csv(cqu_file))

title: Prepare Your Accelerometer Files
text: Now consider dataset just normal CSV file column names chosen researcher sensor software. generic import path helps. Instead forcing rename everything advance, moover can ask columns mean .
code: # Preview the generic example file that ships with the package. generic_file <- system.file("extdata", "generic_example.csv", package = "moover") head(read.csv(generic_file)) # Open the import wizard. # If you choose "Generic delimited files", moover will show a preview # and ask which columns contain time, x, y, z, and id. wizard_import()

what: The easiest path: use the wizard
title: Build Your First Behaviour Model
text: first run, wizard easiest option explains question asking answer . right choice many beginners. also good way learn order steps writing code.
code: library(moover)  # Open the training wizard. # The wizard will guide you through the file paths and settings, # and it can run the full workflow for you. wizard_train()

title: Build Your First Behaviour Model
text: prefer copy-pasteable code, can express training run spec. spec simply records choices want moover use.
code: library(moover)  # Create a run specification for a beginner training run. spec <- create_spec(   workspace = list(root = "my_moover_workspace"),   labels = list(     # tech.csv links animal ids and accelerometer ids when that mapping is needed.     tech_file = "tech.csv",     # observations.csv contains the labelled behaviour periods used for training.     path = "observations.csv"   ),   features = list(     # Use the standard beginner feature set.     selection = "standard",     standard_set = "manual5"   ),   optimise = list(     # For a first model, keep optimisation off.     enabled = FALSE   ),   model = list(     # In a binary model, this is the behaviour treated as the positive class.     positive_class = "grazing"   ) )  # Run the complete workflow from import through export. run_pipeline(spec, stage = "all")

title: Build Your First Behaviour Model
text: model-training run can feel like lot happening behind scenes, helps name main steps plainly. run, moover : read raw accelerometer files standardise timestamps group samples fixed time blocks calculate features block line blocks observed behaviour bouts train validate Random Forest model write export bundle containing fitted model support files full arc training workflow.

title: Build Your First Behaviour Model
text: run, see several outputs. Three useful accuracy, macro F1, confusion matrix. Accuracy tells proportion predictions correct overall. Macro F1 gives class equal weight. useful one behaviour much common another. confusion matrix shows behaviours confused . often informative accuracy alone. moover also reports performance using LOCO, means testing animals model seen training. usually gives realistic sense model behave new animals.

what: What you should expect to find afterwards
title: Build Your First Behaviour Model
text: successful run leaves behind clear trail: run folder inside runs/ results files quality checks plots showing model performance exported model bundle inside run’s models/ folder point need memorise every output file. important thing know model bundle can used later prediction sharing. title: Understand Your Results and Export Folder
text: completed run sits inside runs/<run_id>/. Inside folder usually see several subfolders, including results/, models/, plots/, qc/. useful way think : results/ holds data products run models/ holds model export bundles plots/ holds figures help inspect performance qc/ holds previews checks help confirm data looked sensible

title: Understand Your Results and Export Folder
text: Inside run’s models/ folder, find one exported model bundles. bundle part likely shared collaborators. typical bundle includes: rf_model_full.rds feature_manifest.csv model_spec.json metrics_overall.csv metrics_by_class.csv confusion_matrix.csv test_vectors.csv rf_tree_dump.json

title: Start Here: Your First Successful Run
text: people begin files straight away, often two problems tangled together: learning workflow checking data structure. example workspace separates two jobs. First learn workflow. move files.

what: Find and copy the example workspace
title: Start Here: Your First Successful Run
text: package ships small example workspace. don’t run directly inside installed package folder, installed package files treated read-. Instead, copy example workspace folder can write . point, normal moover workspace machine. next chapter ’ll look folder structure detail. now, just need place run somewhere write outputs.
code: library(moover)  # Find the example workspace that ships inside the moover package. # This folder already contains raw accelerometer files, a tech table, # and an observations table. example_dir <- system.file("extdata", "example_workspace", package = "moover")  # Choose a writable folder on your own computer. # In a real session you would usually point this somewhere permanent, # such as a project folder in Documents. workspace_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "moover_first_run") dir.create(workspace_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)  # Copy the example workspace into that writable location. # After this step, you have your own working copy of the example files. file.copy(   from = list.files(example_dir, full.names = TRUE, all.files = TRUE, no.. = TRUE),   to = workspace_dir,   recursive = TRUE )

title: Start Here: Your First Successful Run
text: Next, create small object stores instructions run. moover calls specification, spec short. need write JSON hand. create_spec() function builds instruction set . first example keep things simple: point moover example workspace tell tech observation files use standard beginner feature set turn optimisation now
code: # Create a simple run specification. # The workspace root is the folder we just copied the example files into. spec <- create_spec(   workspace = list(root = workspace_dir),   labels = list(     # tech.csv links animal ids and accelerometer ids when needed.     tech_file = "tech.csv",     # observations.csv contains the labelled behaviour periods.     path = "observations.csv"   ),   features = list(     # Use the beginner-friendly standard feature set.     selection = "standard",     standard_set = "manual5"   ),   optimise = list(     # Keep the first run simpler and quicker.     enabled = FALSE   ) )

title: Start Here: Your First Successful Run
text: Now can hand spec run_pipeline(). beginner example, stage = "" means: import raw accelerometer files build epoch features match features observations train validate model export finished model bundle rather answer questions interactively write code, can job wizard:
code: # Run the full beginner workflow. # This may still take a little time on a normal computer, and that is expected. run_pipeline(spec, stage = "all") # Open the beginner training wizard. # The wizard explains each question before asking for input. wizard_train()

title: Optimise a Model for Deployment
text: model best possible accuracy always practical model. embedded device, example, may need smaller model even means giving little performance. optimisation really choosing trade-. moover, usually means comparing candidates differ things like: feature subset size number trees minimum node size rolling features

what: The idea of a candidate model
title: Optimise a Model for Deployment
text: moover optimises, commit one model immediately. Instead, trains evaluates multiple candidate models writes tables help compare . words, optimisation answers practical question: model good enough, small enough, job care ?

title: Optimise a Model for Deployment
text: can turn optimisation training wizard, can explicitly code.
code: library(moover)  spec <- create_spec(   workspace = list(root = "my_moover_workspace"),   labels = list(     tech_file = "tech.csv",     path = "observations.csv"   ),   optimise = list(     enabled = TRUE   ) )  # Run only the optimisation stage. optimise_model(spec)

what: How to read the candidate tables
title: Optimise a Model for Deployment
text: optimisation output usually includes candidate tables list, model: accuracy macro F1 model size proxy key model settings good way read tables start deciding care . example: want best possible accuracy? want smallest model threshold? want balanced compromise? decision clear, candidate tables become much easier interpret.

what: What you need before you begin
title: Predict Behaviour with an Existing Model
text: prediction, need: model bundle exported moover folder raw accelerometer files tech.csv file need map logger ids back animal ids cases, model bundle main thing. contains fitted model, feature list, settings needed rebuild features new data.

what: The easiest path: use the prediction wizard
title: Predict Behaviour with an Existing Model
text: prediction wizard good choice want help checking bundle path raw data path running.
code: library(moover)  # Open the prediction wizard. # The wizard explains what each path is for before asking for input. wizard_predict()

title: Predict Behaviour with an Existing Model
text: prediction run can also written spec. , important choice model_bundle path.
code: library(moover)  spec <- create_spec(   workspace = list(root = "my_moover_workspace"),   ingest = list(     # Point to the folder containing the new raw accelerometer files.     raw_dir = "data_raw"   ),   labels = list(     # Supply tech.csv if you need it for id mapping.     tech_file = "tech.csv",     # No observation file is needed for prediction-only work.     path = NULL   ),   predict = list(     # This should point to the exported model folder.     model_bundle = "path/to/exported_model_bundle",     # Keep the default epoch-level predictions.     summary_outputs = character()   ) )  # Run prediction using the existing model bundle. run_pipeline(spec, stage = "predict")

title: Predict Behaviour with an Existing Model
text: Prediction shorter model training, logic similar. moover still needs take raw movement data feature-building process used training. practice, : read new raw data standardise timestamps build fixed time blocks used model calculate features expected model bundle write epoch-level predictions class probabilities key idea consistency. model useful new data features rebuilt way.

what: One row means one observed bout
title: Record Observations for Training
text: easiest way think observation file : row describes one labelled behaviour period one animal. example, animal grazing 10:00:00 10:03:00, one row. animal resting 10:03:00 10:08:00, another row.

title: Record Observations for Training
text: training time, moover needs know four things row: animal observation belongs behaviour label observed behaviour started behaviour ended simple example. table deliberately simple. file, columns can different names map spec, four ideas must still present.
code: obs_example <- data.frame(   id = c("Cow_01", "Cow_01", "Cow_02"),   label = c("grazing", "resting", "grazing"),   start_unix_ms = c(1712128800000, 1712128980000, 1712128800000),   end_unix_ms = c(1712128980000, 1712129280000, 1712129100000) )  knitr::kable(obs_example)

title: Record Observations for Training

title: Scripted Reruns and Saved Specs
text: people run workflow interactively, often remember broad choices forget exact settings. saved spec solves problem. records file locations settings form can reused. means spec useful least three reasons: can rerun workflow later can share run instructions colleague can move interactive work scripted work without changing underlying pipeline

title: Scripted Reruns and Saved Specs
text: simple example spec training workflow. spec, can rerun workflow directly.
code: library(moover)  spec <- create_spec(   workspace = list(root = "my_moover_workspace"),   labels = list(     tech_file = "tech.csv",     path = "observations.csv"   ),   features = list(     selection = "standard",     standard_set = "manual5"   ),   optimise = list(enabled = FALSE) ) run_pipeline(spec, stage = "all")

title: Scripted Reruns and Saved Specs
text: moover also writes specs JSON runs can reproduced later without stepping wizard . need hand-edit JSON benefit . beginners can think JSON file saved record choices already made.
code: # Re-run a workflow from a previously saved JSON spec. run_pipeline("runs/20260330_012345/spec/run_spec.json", stage = "all")

what: When the import preview looks wrong
title: Troubleshooting and Glossary
text: import preview look sensible, stop fix import anything else. Typical causes : wrong timestamp format chosen wrong columns mapped generic file ids raw data ids expected preview stage exists precisely can catch problems early.

what: When training fails or behaves strangely
title: Troubleshooting and Glossary
text: training runs results look suspicious, common causes usually inputs rather Random Forest settings. Things check first: observation ids match raw-data ids? observation times right timezone format? behaviour labels spelled consistently? actually enough labelled examples behaviour? checks usually productive changing model settings random.

title: Troubleshooting and Glossary
text: prediction run finishes outputs make sense, ask whether new data really matches conditions model expects. example: type accelerometer data? ids mapped correctly? Can features rebuilt new files? Unexpected predictions automatically mean model broken. Often mean new dataset aligned training setup.

title: Troubleshooting and Glossary
text: Accelerometer file: raw movement data recorded device. Canonical format: standard 5-column layout moover uses internally: id, t_unix_ms, x, y, z. Epoch: fixed time block. moover builds features block. Feature: numeric summary calculated movement data within epoch. Model bundle: exported folder containing fitted model supporting files. LOCO: leave-one-cow-validation, meaning model tested animals used training. Spec: saved set instructions run.

title: Troubleshooting and Glossary
text: good moment ask help answered three questions: chapter following? file step seems causing trouble? preview output look like went wrong? details usually make much easier someone else help quickly.
